attendee: Pankaj Chandak (with Jeff Hoo) · Granite Investment Partners

execs.name: Steve Anderson
execs.title: Senior Vice President of Administration and Investor Relations

brief.what_they_do: Global maker of agricultural equipment — high-horsepower tractors, combines, and precision-ag technology (brands: Fendt, Massey Ferguson, Valtra, PTx). [book]

company_brief.the_view: A big Q1 earnings beat in a still-soft ag market — adj EPS $0.94 vs $0.44 consensus [book], on sales of $2,342.9M (+14.3% y/y, but only +4.7% ex-FX) [book]. The beat was driven by strong Europe (EME) margins, FX, a lower tax rate, and channel revenue that ran ahead of end-user demand — i.e. quality is mixed, not pure demand. Management RAISED the FY26 adj-EPS outlook to ~$6.00 (from $5.50–6.00) [book], hiked the dividend to $0.30, and announced $350M of Q2 buybacks. Yet the stock reaction was negative — the debate is whether the 1Q beat is durable or front-loaded, against a higher tariff bill (~$135M in 2026) and a softer Q2.

rows: GM 24.8% vs 25.4% — (60) bps; cost inputs incl. tariffs [book]
Adj OM 4.6% vs 4.1% — +50 bps; EME the driver [book]
FX + lower tax + EME volume leverage; ~$0.50 of the beat [book]
pnl.read: a headline blowout, but read the quality: of the ~$0.50 beat, much is FX, tax, and channel revenue that 'ran ahead of end-user demand' [book]. Underlying ag demand is still soft (NA & Brazil retail tractor units down y/y); the real earnings power is the EME margin and the structural-cost program, not a demand recovery.

company_brief.guidance: FY26 RAISED: adj EPS ~$6.00 (prior $5.50–6.00; ~$5.77 cons) [book]; net sales $10.5–10.7bn; adj op margin guide up. But it embeds a higher tariff cost of ~$135M in 2026 (+$90M y/y, and $25M above the prior estimate) on the Section 232 methodology change [book], and a softer Q2 (2Q EPS guide below the 1Q run-rate). The raise is mostly the 1Q beat + buyback flow-through, not a demand-led upgrade.
company_brief.sell_side: Split desk on the SAME print [book]: Jefferies Buy $145 · JPMorgan Overweight $143 · Morgan Stanley Overweight · BMO Market Perform · Baird Neutral $120. Bulls: EME margin resilience, structural transformation, capital return, 'stress-case' valuation. Bears: beat is FX/tax/timing-aided, tariff headwind rising, demand still down, Q2 softer — hence the negative stock reaction despite the beat.

room.note: Pankaj's 2on1 meeting is with the CFO (the CEO is NOT in this room).

people.name: Damon Audia
people.title: Chief Financial Officer & SVP
people.tells_from_call: On the Q1 call, Audia framed the tariff math precisely — ~$135M 2026 tariff cost, +$90M y/y, $25M above prior estimate (Section 232) [book] — and tied the raised guide to the 1Q beat + buyback, not demand. A numbers-forward, bridge-the-walk CFO; he volunteers the offsets (tariffs, mix) rather than only the positives. [book]
last_q_commitments: ~$135M FY26 tariff cost; could change as the year evolves [book]
$350M buyback to commence in Q2; dividend raised to $0.30 [book]
Continued dealer/company inventory alignment and structural cost savings [book]
people.bio: AGCO SVP & CFO since **July 1, 2022** (succeeded Andy Beck, AGCO's CFO of 20 years). A seasoned industrials CFO with **two prior public-company CFO seats** — Kennametal (2018–2022) and Carpenter Technology (2015–2018) — plus ~10 years at Goodyear (ending as SVP Finance, North America) and earlier finance roles at Delphi/GM. MBA, Carnegie Mellon; undergrad, University of Michigan. [web]

not_in_room.name: Eric Hansotia
not_in_room.title: Chairman, President & CEO
not_in_room.why_relevant: Drove the prepared-remarks narrative (Farmer-First, precision ag, market-share gains in high-HP). Bio researched for context even though not in the room.
not_in_room.bio: Chairman, President & CEO since **Jan 1, 2021** (succeeded Martin Richenhagen). Joined AGCO in 2013 (initially leading its precision-ag / Fuse connected-services push), rose to SVP & COO, then CEO; the **architect of the 'Farmer-First' strategy**. Spent a ~20-year career at John Deere before AGCO (global engineering / product management, incl. a Mannheim, Germany posting). BSME + MSME, University of Wisconsin–Madison; MBA, University of Iowa. [web]

smart_questions.q: How much of the 1Q adjusted EPS beat ($0.94 vs $0.44) was channel revenue that 'ran ahead of end-user demand,' and does that reverse in 2H — i.e. did you pull forward sales rather than see demand inflect?
smart_questions.why: Brokers flagged rev-rec timing as part of the beat; this is the durability question. [book]
smart_questions.listen_for: Good: a hard number on the pull-forward + a sign demand actually inflected. Evasive: 'timing normalizes through the year' with no figure → the beat was borrowed from 2H and reverses.

smart_questions.q: The FY26 tariff cost jumped to ~$135M (+$25M vs your prior estimate) on the Section 232 change. What's the offset path — price, sourcing, or margin absorption — and is $135M now conservative or still moving?
smart_questions.why: Tariffs are the rising headwind partly offsetting the beat in the bridge. [book]
smart_questions.listen_for: Good: a concrete offset bridge (price X, sourcing Y) and '$135M is our conservative number.' Evasive: 'we're monitoring it' → the headwind is still open and the guide is at risk.

smart_questions.q: EME carried the quarter (margin ~16%, +$104.6M op income y/y) while North America op income fell. How sustainable is EME margin, and what inflects NA from here?
smart_questions.why: Regional mix is the whole quality-of-beat debate. [book]
smart_questions.listen_for: Good: a structural reason EME holds (mix, Fendt, cost program) + a dated NA inflection. Evasive: leaning on FX/comps → the margin is cyclical, not structural, and concentration risk is real.

smart_questions.q: You raised the dividend and announced $350M of Q2 buybacks plus a ~$190M JV stake sale to Rabobank funding repurchases — how should we think about capital-return pace vs. reinvestment given still-soft demand?
smart_questions.why: Capital return is a key bull pillar; the JV monetization is new. [book]
smart_questions.listen_for: Good: a clear capital-allocation framework + whether the JV sale is repeatable. Evasive: vague on pace → buyback is opportunistic optics, not a committed lever.

smart_questions.q: Retail tractor units are still down in NA and Brazil. What's your read on when the ag down-cycle troughs, and what's the first sign you'd point us to?
smart_questions.why: The cycle call underlies every estimate; ask the CFO for the leading indicator. [book]
smart_questions.listen_for: Good: a specific leading indicator he watches (dealer inventory weeks, used-equipment pricing, order book). Evasive: 'hard to call' → no visibility, and 2026 estimates rest on faith.

granite_pov.line: A high-quality franchise printing a low-quality-of-source beat: own the EME margin and structural-cost story, but the 1Q upside is FX + tax + channel timing, not a demand turn, and the tariff bill is climbing. The capital-return step-up (dividend + $350M buyback + JV monetization) is the cleanest positive. Watch the rev-rec reversal risk into 2H and the ag-cycle trough signal.

brief.sourcing: All financials [book] = AGCO FactSet Public Information Book (Q1 2026: press release, transcript, 5 broker notes). Numbers recomputed from the book's financial schedule. Exec bios [web] (bio only; no insider data in v1).

task2_guidance.posture: Cautious raise — the FY guide went up but mostly on the 1Q beat + buyback, not a demand upgrade; it absorbs a higher tariff bill. [book]

task2_guidance.read: holding-to-slightly-raising after a 129% EPS beat IS the signal: management is telling you the 1Q upside is timing (FX, tax, channel), and a rising tariff bill + softer Q2 caps the full-year flow-through. [book]

task3_segments.title: Task 3 · Regional segments
task3_segments.note: AGCO reports by region, not product segment — table adapts. [book]

Brazil weak — the soft spot; retail units down [book]
task3_segments.read: this is a one-region quarter: EME margin (16.2%) carried the whole beat while North America swung to a (12.5)% op margin and LATAM stayed deeply negative. The bull case lives or dies on EME holding. [book]

task4_kpis.title: Task 4 · KPIs

rows: Funded partly by ~$190M Rabobank JV stake sale [book]
task4_kpis.read: the operating KPIs say the cycle hasn't turned — retail units down in NA + Brazil; the beat is margin/cost execution + capital return, not unit demand. [book]

bullish: EME margin resilience (16.2%) + the structural-cost transformation is delivering [book]
Capital-return step-up: dividend raise + $350M buyback + ~$190M JV monetization [book]
FY26 EPS guide raised to ~$6.00, ~4% above the ~$5.77 consensus [book]
Several desks see 'stress-case' valuation — downside seen as limited [book]
Jefferies Buy $145 / JPM Overweight $143 / Morgan Stanley Overweight [book]
bearish: The beat is FX + tax + channel revenue that ran ahead of end demand — low quality of source [book]
FY26 tariff cost rising to ~$135M (+$25M vs prior) on Section 232 [book]
North America op margin swung to (12.5)%; demand still soft [book]
Implied softer Q2 vs the 1Q run-rate [book]
BMO Market Perform / Baird Neutral $120 — weak farmer sentiment risk [book]
task6_sellside.read: the desk is split on the SAME print — bulls buy the margin/capital-return story, bears flag a low-quality beat into a rising tariff bill. That split is exactly why the stock fell on a beat. [book]

task7_stock.title: Task 7 · Stock reaction
drivers: Negative reaction DESPITE the beat + raise — the market read the beat as FX/tax/channel-timing, not demand [book]
Rising tariff headwind (~$135M) + a softer implied Q2 undercut the raise [book]
Broader ag-sentiment / macro overhang weighed regardless of the print [book]
task7_stock.read: a 'sell the beat' quarter: the print was good but low-quality, the raise was mechanical (beat + buyback), and the cycle hasn't turned — so the stock faded. [book]

triggers: Watch 2H for whether the 'ran-ahead-of-demand' channel revenue reverses (de-stock).
Watch the tariff number — flagged as still moving (Section 232).
new_debates: Rabobank JV monetization — is this a one-off or a recurring capital-efficiency lever?
Does the structural-cost program offset a still-soft demand cycle long enough to matter?
drivers_autodraft.updated_pov: AUTO-DRAFT working view: a high-quality franchise printing a low-quality-of-source beat. Bull pillars = EME margin + capital return; bear pillars = demand cycle hasn't turned + rising tariffs + the beat's composition. The room question is durability: press the CFO on the 2H channel-reversal risk. (Needs Pankaj to confirm/replace as a real Driver.)
